title: Aguinaldo Calculator Argentina 2026 — SAC Net Pay | Hacé Cuentas
description: Calculate your Argentine aguinaldo (13th-month bonus): gross SAC based on best salary of the semester, 17% deductions, and proportional months worked.
intro: The **Sueldo Anual Complementario (SAC)**, commonly known as **aguinaldo**, is a mandatory 13th-month bonus for all employees in Argentina. It equals **50% of the highest monthly gross salary earned during the semester** and is paid in two installments: before June 30 (first aguinaldo) and before December 18 (second aguinaldo). This calculator computes the **proportional aguinaldo** based on months worked, subtracts the standard 17% social security contributions (pension + healthcare + PAMI), and shows you the net amount you'll receive. It works for employees who completed the full semester, those who started mid-year, and final settlements in case of termination or resignation.
keyTakeaway: **Aguinaldo = best gross salary of the semester / 2 x (months worked / 6)**. Paid in June and December, subject to the same 17% social security deductions as your regular salary.
useCases: You're about to receive your aguinaldo and want to know the net amount hitting your account.
You started working mid-year and need to calculate the proportional amount.
You were terminated or resigned and need to verify the proportional SAC owed to you.
You earned a bonus in one month of the semester and want to check if it counts toward the aguinaldo.
You're an accountant or HR professional who needs a quick calculation for payroll settlements.

fields.label: Best gross salary of the semester
fields.help: The highest gross monthly salary earned during the semester

explanation:
## How the Aguinaldo Is Calculated in Argentina

The **Sueldo Anual Complementario (SAC)** is regulated by Articles 121 and 122 of Argentina's Labor Contract Law (LCT 20.744) and Law 27.073, which splits it into two semi-annual payments.

### Formula
```
SAC = (best_gross_salary_of_semester / 2) x (months_worked / 6)
```

## Payment Dates

| Installment | Period Covered | Deadline |
|---|---|---|
| First aguinaldo | January - June | **June 30** (with 4 business day grace period) |
| Second aguinaldo | July - December | **December 18** |

## What Counts as 'Best Salary'

The calculation uses the **highest gross monthly remuneration of the semester**, including:
- Base salary (as per collective agreement or individual contract)
- Remunerative bonuses: seniority, attendance, punctuality
- **Overtime** if it was regular during the semester
- **Commissions** (semester average if variable)
- Zone allowances, on-call pay

NOT included:
- Non-remunerative per diems or travel allowances
- One-time extraordinary bonuses
- Tips (unless regular and declared)
- Social benefits (childcare, meal vouchers)

## Deductions on the Aguinaldo

The aguinaldo is subject to the same social security deductions as regular monthly salary:
- **Pension (SIPA)**: 11%
- **Healthcare (Obra Social)**: 3%
- **PAMI (retiree health)**: 3%

If your cumulative annual income exceeds the income tax threshold (MNI), the aguinaldo is also subject to income tax using a special proration method.

## Proportional Aguinaldo: When It Applies

- **Started mid-semester**: you receive a proportional amount for months actually worked.
- **Terminated without cause**: you receive a proportional amount up to the date of termination (part of the final settlement).
- **Resigned**: you also receive the proportional amount.
- **Maternity leave**: months on maternity count toward SAC since the employment relationship remains active.
- **Unpaid leave**: does NOT count toward SAC (no remuneration accrued).

## Common Mistakes

1. **Using the last salary instead of the best one**: if you got a raise during the semester, the aguinaldo is based on the higher amount.
2. **Not counting bonuses and overtime**: if they were regular, they count.
3. **Forgetting the proportional calculation**: employers don't always get it right -- verify it yourself.
4. **Thinking non-remunerative bonuses don't affect SAC**: if a bonus is classified as remunerative, it does count.

faq.q: When is the aguinaldo paid in Argentina?
faq.a: In two installments: **the first before June 30** (with a 4 business day grace period) and **the second before December 18**. If the deadline falls on a non-business day, it's paid the previous business day. This is regulated by LCT and Law 27.073.

faq.q: Do I get an aguinaldo if I only worked 3 months?
faq.a: Yes. The aguinaldo is paid **proportional to the months actually worked**. If you worked 3 out of 6 months in the semester, you receive 3/6 (50%) of the full SAC amount.

faq.q: How is the 'best salary' of the semester determined?
faq.a: It's the **highest gross monthly remuneration** you earned in any month of the semester, including base salary + remunerative bonuses + regular overtime. If you received a raise mid-semester, the post-raise salary is used. If you earned a remunerative bonus in one month, that month's total is used.

faq.q: What deductions apply to the aguinaldo?
faq.a: The same as regular monthly salary: **17% fixed** (pension 11% + healthcare 3% + PAMI 3%). If your annual income exceeds the income tax threshold (MNI), income tax is also deducted using a special proration formula.

faq.q: Does the aguinaldo pay income tax?
faq.a: It depends: if your monthly salary already pays income tax, the aguinaldo is also subject to it using a 'proration' methodology (spread across the months from January). If you don't pay income tax on your salary, the aguinaldo doesn't either.

faq.q: What happens to the aguinaldo if I'm terminated?
faq.a: You're entitled to **proportional SAC** for the months worked until the date of termination. It's included in the final settlement along with severance pay, notice period pay, unused vacation, and worked days of the current month.

faq.q: Do freelancers (monotributo) or self-employed workers receive aguinaldo?
faq.a: No. The aguinaldo is an exclusive right of **employees in a formal employment relationship** under the Labor Contract Law (LCT). Freelancers and self-employed workers under monotributo do not receive SAC.

faq.q: Do part-time or hourly workers get an aguinaldo?
faq.a: Yes, they do. It's calculated on the **best gross remuneration of the semester**, whether part-time or full-time. The proportion formula is the same (50% x months worked / 6).

example.title: Example: Started in March, June aguinaldo
steps: **Months worked** in the Jan-Jun semester: 4 (March, April, May, June).
**Best gross salary** of the semester: ARS 1,800,000.
example.result: You're entitled to a gross aguinaldo of **ARS 600,000** and a net (take-home) of **ARS 498,000** for the 4 months worked.
